title: A note on local inference when reliability turned out to be the whole game
summary: A post about local inference when reliability turned out to be the whole game, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: local inference when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: My notes on local inference began with a small irritation in November 2024. Once everyone began treating the story as self-evident, I started looking for what had been flattened. When a formulation travels instantly, I assume some awkward but important detail got left behind. In this case the omitted piece was the long argument between autonomy and auditability. The abstract version vanishes as soon as you track who has to maintain the thing, who bears its failures, and who gains from the trust it extracts.

Local inference appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. Talking about the engineering is straightforward because it collapses cleanly into diagrams, benchmarks, and staged proof. The fantasy half is harder to measure, but it matters just as much. Every tool drags along a picture of its preferred user and a social setting it would like to stabilize. Those assumptions are rarely hidden, they are etched into the design decisions. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the behavioral atmosphere the system smuggles in under the banner of functionality.

Again and again I ran into the difference between a prototype setting and a public one. Prototype settings come with a crucial honesty: everyone knows it is still an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. The first audience is often self-selected for novelty, which makes the test environment gentler than public life. It is another thing to build the same logic into institutions and hope design language can hide the cost. The long argument between autonomy and auditability becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

My taste for the subject stayed mostly intact, but my threshold for abstraction fell sharply. After writing “The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not expect it to last. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What matters to me is proportion, not the fantasy of purity. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. Still, it matters to notice when a useful tool crosses the line into quiet obligation. Local inference seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often the shift is relational before it is technological, and it becomes normal before it becomes examined.

A final note to myself from November 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. At this point being right about the future matters less than being honest about who carries the burden. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous framing is usually what keeps the important part out of view.

references.title: The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
On public health dashboards when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
